dossier.overview:
Overworld props (buildings, signs, decorations) are authored as **GLB** (glTF 2.0 binary) and rendered by both the Operations Desk preview and the Pokémon Resort C++ game. Source art is usually a DS rip exported as **OBJ + MTL + PNG** and zipped; the Operations Desk converts it to a self-contained GLB, stores it under `pokemon-resort/assets/overworld/models/<id>/`, and lets you place it on a map. The same GLB feeds the in-engine renderer.

The single most important rule of this pipeline is how it treats **UV orientation** and **texture transparency**. DS rips combine an orange-roof / grey-glass atlas with a partly-transparent alpha channel, and getting either wrong produces the classic failure modes: a black roof, a torn roof, or a black banner. This article documents the conversion rules, the measured-alpha policy that fixes those, the Map Editor placement flow, and the C++ loader/renderer.

Canonical code: `pokemon-resort-page/tools/admin/lib/{obj-compile,write-glb,texture-alpha}.mjs` (conversion) and `pokemon-resort/src/gameplay/world3d/{data/GlbModelLoader,rendering/GlbModelRenderer}.cpp` (runtime). In-repo reference: `pokemon-resort/docs/gameplay/overworld_glb_models.md`.

sections.title: Where GLB props fit
sections.summary: OBJ zip → Operations Desk converter → GLB on disk → preview, placement, and game.

blocks.body: Authors upload a `.zip` (OBJ + MTL + textures) or a ready `.glb` in **Operations Desk → Models**. OBJ uploads run through the converter; GLB uploads are stored as-is. Either way the result is a self-contained GLB plus a `model.json` manifest (footprint, AABB, triangle/vertex counts, material names). The Map Editor reads that catalog to place props into an `.owmap`, and the C++ game loads the referenced GLB at runtime.

blocks.title: Authoring pipeline
blocks.caption: One conversion path; the GLB on disk is the single source of truth for preview and game.
blocks.source:
flowchart LR
    Z[OBJ + MTL + PNG zip] -->|buildObjMeshFromUpload| M[Compiled mesh + textures]
    M -->|writeGlbFromMesh| G[Self-contained .glb]
    G --> J[model.json manifest]
    G --> P[Operations Desk preview - three.js]
    G --> E[Map Editor placement -> .owmap models array]
    E --> C[C++ GlbModelLoader -> GlbModelRenderer]

sections.title: OBJ → GLB conversion rules
sections.summary: UV orientation, one material per MTL, sampling, and the measured-alpha policy.

blocks.body:
The converter requires authored OBJ texture coordinates (`vt`) on at least 85% of faces — it never bakes, normalizes, or clamps UVs. The **one transform** applied is a vertical flip:

**V flip — `v → 1 − v`.** OBJ texcoords use a bottom-left origin; glTF (and the SDL renderer) use a top-left origin. Without the flip the model samples a vertically mirrored atlas, which on the Pokémon Center reads as a *black roof* (the orange roof region and the black region are swapped top-to-bottom). With the flip, big faces sample the same texels external OBJ viewers show.

blocks.caption: obj-compile.mjs — interleave position/normal/UV with the V flip

blocks.html:
RuleBehaviour
One glTF material per MTL materialEach gets its own embedded PNG/JPEG; primitives are grouped per material slot
SamplerNearest filtering, REPEAT wrap (DS UVs are often outside 0..1)
Vertex layoutInterleaved POSITION (3) + NORMAL (3) + TEXCOORD_0 (2) = 8 floats
Self-containedSingle BIN chunk; textures embedded as bufferViews — no external URIs

blocks.body:
DS-ripped PNGs are RGBA, but the alpha channel is **usually fully opaque** and only *occasionally* a genuine cutout. Two naive policies both fail:

• **"Has an alpha channel → MASK"** punches holes in solid surfaces, because most DS PNGs ship an opaque alpha that some tools fill with zeros over real content.
• **"Force everything OPAQUE"** turns a real cutout (a banner / sign with a transparent background) into a **black square**.

The correct policy is to **decode the alpha channel and measure it**. `texture-alpha.mjs` inflates and unfilters the PNG, then counts transparent texels. A material becomes `alphaMode: MASK` (with `alphaCutoff: 0.5`) only when at least 0.5% of texels are actually transparent; otherwise it stays `OPAQUE`. The 0.5 cutoff matches DS 1-bit alpha and gives crisp edges. Because the V flip is correct, opaque regions (the orange roof) keep full alpha and are never cut, while the banner's transparent background is cut instead of rendered black.

blocks.caption: texture-alpha.mjs — measure real transparency, not just channel presence
write-glb.mjs — MASK only where measured transparency exists

blocks.html:
Worked example — Pokémon Center (4 materials), after decoding each texture's alpha:

MaterialTextureMeasured alphaExported mode
h_kage (shadow)16×16100% opaqueOPAQUE
pc_1 (walls)64×64100% opaqueOPAQUE
light_a (lights/banner)64×64~33% transparentMASK (cutoff 0.5)
pc_2 (roof + banner)64×64~33% transparentMASK (cutoff 0.5)

Note pc_2 covers both the opaque orange roof and transparent banner texels; MASK cuts only the transparent ones, so the roof stays solid.

sections.title: Preview & placement
sections.summary: three.js preview honours the GLB verbatim; placement writes to the .owmap models array.

blocks.body:
**Preview** (`model-glb-viewer.js`) loads the on-disk GLB with `GLTFLoader` and renders with neutral image-based lighting (`RoomEnvironment` IBL + ambient/directional, sRGB output, no tone mapping) so PBR materials read like online glTF viewers. Without lights or an environment, `MeshStandardMaterial` renders **black** — that was an early false alarm mistaken for a texture bug.

`model-texture-alpha.js` only tunes sampling (nearest + REPEAT, double-sided) and takes alpha **verbatim** from the GLB material's `alphaMode` (GLTFLoader sets `transparent`/`alphaTest`). It deliberately does **not** re-derive transparency from the texture's alpha channel in the browser — that decision now lives entirely in the converter.

**Placement.** In the Map Editor, pick a prop, click **Place**, then click grid tiles. While placing, a translucent **ghost** (footprint outline + roof image) follows the cursor so you can see exactly which cells and shape the prop will take before clicking. Each placement is appended to the map's `models[]` array. Per-placement controls rotate (90°), scale, and remove. Saving bakes into the `.owmap` metadata.

**Footprint + roof on the 2D grid.** Placed props highlight **every cell** of their rotated footprint (catalog `w×d`, swapped for 90/270 yaw) and overlay the model's **roof/top snapshot** image over those cells, so the paint grid reads as a true top-down layout rather than a single origin marker.

**Workspace 2D / 3D toggle.** A 2D / 3D switch in the tool rail flips the whole workspace. 2D is the paint grid above. **3D is a view-only scene** that renders a height-shaded terrain mesh plus every placed prop as its **actual GLB** under an orbit camera (drag to orbit, scroll to zoom). Painting and placement stay in 2D; 3D is for checking how the laid-out map reads in three dimensions.

**Card thumbnails.** Each catalog card renders a real front-face snapshot of the model (a one-shot orthographic GLB render to a transparent PNG, cached per id+hash) plus its tile footprint and area (`w×d · N tiles`).

**Fixing orientation (re-bake).** Some rips import lying on their side or upside-down (a truck that comes in front-side-down). The model preview has X / Y / Z buttons that rotate in 90° steps with a live preview; **Save orientation** bakes that rotation into the GLB itself (`/api/overworld-models/reorient` → `reorient-glb.mjs`): it rotates every vertex and normal, re-centers the prop on X/Z, and re-seats it onto the ground plane, then regenerates the manifest (footprint, AABB). Because the correction lives in the geometry, the preview, the Map Editor footprint, and the C++ game all agree — there is no per-instance orientation field to keep in sync.

blocks.html:
models[] entry in .owmap metadata

FieldTypeDescription
idstringCatalog model id
glbstringPath to the placed GLB (project-root relative)
position[x, y, z]World position; y = height level × tileSize
yawDegnumberRotation about +Y in degrees
scalenumberUniform scale

sections.title: C++ loader & renderer
sections.summary: Self-contained GLB parse, baked transforms, and SDL_RenderGeometry with painter sort.

blocks.body:
`GlbModelLoader` parses the GLB's JSON + BIN chunks, walks the node graph baking world transforms into vertices, and produces a triangulated `GlbMesh` grouped per material with embedded base-color image bytes. It supports indices `u8/u16/u32`, `POSITION`/`TEXCOORD_0`, `baseColorFactor`, and `alphaMode`. External buffers/URIs are rejected — re-export self-contained.

The loader maps `alphaMode` to a single `alpha_blend` flag: both `MASK` and `BLEND` enable blending. There is no separate `alphaCutoff` path in SDL — see the next block.

blocks.caption: GlbModelLoader.cpp — alphaMode → alpha_blend
GlbModelRenderer.cpp — nearest scaling + blend mode from the material
blocks.body:
SDL_SetTextureScaleMode(raw, SDL_ScaleModeNearest);
SDL_SetTextureBlendMode(raw, mat.alpha_blend ? SDL_BLENDMODE_BLEND : SDL_BLENDMODE_NONE);

`SDL_RenderGeometry` has no depth buffer or true alpha test, so the renderer applies the per-placement transform (scale → yaw about +Y → translate), projects via `Gen4FollowCamera`, and **painter-sorts triangles** before drawing. With DS 1-bit alpha and nearest sampling, `SDL_BLENDMODE_BLEND` yields a clean cutout that matches the converter's MASK intent.

**Two-pass transparency order.** Within a model, all **opaque** triangles draw first (back-to-front), then all **cutout** (MASK/BLEND) triangles draw last (back-to-front). Without a depth buffer, a cutout banner triangle nearly coplanar with the opaque wall behind it could tie or flip on centroid depth and let the wall paint over it — the cause of *cut-off banner sections*. Drawing cutout faces last guarantees they composite over the body.

**Emulating REPEAT wrap.** `SDL_RenderGeometry` *clamps* texcoords to 0..1 instead of repeating, and DS UVs routinely exceed 1 (some faces span several tiles). Before drawing, each triangle is clipped against the integer UV grid and every resulting piece is re-based into 0..1, so each piece samples a single texture tile and the clamp becomes a no-op — wrapping textures look identical to the browser preview.

**Scene render order.** Placed props and the player/follower are sorted together by the camera depth of each one's ground anchor and drawn far-to-near, so a character walking behind a building is correctly hidden by it rather than always drawing on top. Each model's anchor is biased toward the camera by a configurable distance (`config/gameplay/world3d/render.json` → `occlusion.modelBehindBiasTiles`, default 1 tile) so a building starts occluding the character *before* its footprint center — e.g. a character entering a doorway is hidden by the building instead of drawing over the roof.

blocks.title: Runtime decode → draw
blocks.caption: Transforms are baked at load; only placement transform + camera vary per frame.
blocks.source:
flowchart TD
    F[.glb on disk] --> L[GlbModelLoader: JSON+BIN, bake node transforms]
    L --> ME[GlbMesh: per-material triangles + image bytes]
    ME --> UP[Lazy IMG_Load_RW upload, nearest, blend if MASK/BLEND]
    UP --> TR[Per-placement scale -> yaw -> translate]
    TR --> PR[Gen4FollowCamera project]
    PR --> SO[Painter sort back-to-front]
    SO --> RG[SDL_RenderGeometry]

sections.title: Troubleshooting & known pitfalls
sections.summary: The symptoms this pipeline was built to avoid, and what each one means.

blocks.html:
SymptomRoot causeFix / where
Whole model renders black in previewNo lights/environment in the three.js scene; MeshStandardMaterial needs IBLmodel-glb-viewer.js studio lighting (already in place)
Roof black (geometry/UVs look fine)Missing V flip — atlas sampled vertically mirroredobj-compile.mjs 1 - uv[1]
Roof torn / diagonal holesMaterial set to MASK while sampling a zero-alpha region (pre-flip)Correct V flip + measured-alpha policy
Banner is a black squareA real cutout forced to OPAQUE (the failure mode of many online converters)Measured-alpha MASK in write-glb.mjs
Solid surface gets holes"Has alpha channel → MASK" over-triggerUse pngHasMeaningfulTransparency (fraction threshold), never channel presence
Prop renders as a solid black blob / smeared seams in game (fine in preview)SDL_RenderGeometry clamps UVs to [0,1] and ignores REPEAT wrap; DS UVs exceed 1 (some faces span multiple tiles), so faces sample the wrong edge texelClip each triangle to the integer UV grid and re-base every piece into [0,1) in GlbModelRenderer.cpp (clipAxis/emitPiece); mirrors OverworldMapRenderer's wrapUv
Character always draws on top of a building, even when behind itModels were drawn as a block before charactersUnified depth sort of props + player + follower by ground-anchor camera depth in Overworld3DTestScreen.cpp (GlbModelRenderer::anchorDepth)
Transparent banner / sign has cut-off sections in game (fine in preview)Cutout triangle nearly coplanar with the opaque wall behind it ties/flips on centroid depth, so the wall paints over itTwo-pass sort in GlbModelRenderer.cpp: opaque first, cutout (MASK/BLEND) last, each back-to-front (DrawTri.cutout)
Character draws over the roof when entering a doorwayBuilding's occlusion anchor is its footprint center, so it only hides the character past the centerIncrease occlusion.modelBehindBiasTiles in config/gameplay/world3d/render.json (biases the anchor forward toward the camera)
Re-oriented prop floats or sinks / off-centerRotation baked without re-seatingreorient-glb.mjs re-centers X/Z and re-seats to y=0 after rotating
Prop missing in game, present in previewGLB referenced an external buffer/URIRe-export self-contained (single BIN chunk)

blocks.body: **Verifying a conversion.** Decode the alpha histogram of each texture and sample the biggest faces' texels; compare against a known-good external viewer (or a reference GLB from an online tool). Equal sampled colours on the big faces confirm the atlas mapping (V orientation) is correct. The alpha histogram (% at 0 vs 255) confirms which materials should be MASK vs OPAQUE. DS textures are effectively 1-bit alpha (clusters at 0 and 255, little in between).

**Server reloads.** The Operations Desk is a plain Node process with no hot reload. After changing any `tools/admin/lib/*.mjs` module (including the converter), **restart `npm run admin`** before importing again. The browser preview reads the on-disk GLB, so a re-generated model previews correctly without a restart, but a fresh conversion through the UI will use the old code until the server restarts.
